jokes.text: Will, give me all your money. / I'm sorry, Carlton. I don't feel like playing 7-Eleven.
jokes.setup: Carlton's straightforward demand for money
jokes.punchline: Will frames it as playing convenience store robbery, turning Carlton's request into a street game
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character comedy that instantly establishes the dynamic. Will's street perspective reframes Carlton's privileged request as playing robbery - perfect economy and character integration.

jokes.text: $50? I could take 25 women out to dinner.
jokes.setup: Carlton needs $50 for investment
jokes.punchline: Will's math implies $2 dates, revealing his cheap approach to romance
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Will's street-smart penny-pinching vs Carlton's investment mindset.

jokes.text: And I know you can hear me with ears that big.
jokes.setup: Carlton trying to get Will's attention
jokes.punchline: Casual insult about Will's ears
jokes.explanation: Standard insult humor, functional but not particularly clever or original.

jokes.text: So is that French nanny you're dating. Enough said.
jokes.setup: Carlton mentions illegal inside trading
jokes.punchline: Will implies Carlton's dating an underage nanny, deflating Carlton's moral high ground
jokes.explanation: Sharp comeback that subverts Carlton's moral authority with dark humor. Great economy - 'enough said' sells the joke perfectly.

jokes.text: Will, take my hand... come out of the ghetto... and take a stroll down Wall Street.
jokes.setup: Carlton trying to convince Will to invest
jokes.punchline: Carlton's condescending, overly dramatic invitation treats Wall Street like enlightenment
jokes.explanation: Perfect Carlton pomposity - the theatrical hand-taking and 'come out of the ghetto' phrasing is peak privileged Carlton.

jokes.text: Now, when you take a girl out to dinner, they can get something to eat, too.
jokes.setup: Carlton explaining the profits from their investment
jokes.punchline: Carlton suggests Will currently can't afford to feed his dates
jokes.explanation: Solid callback to Will's cheap dating habits, though Carlton delivering this line feels slightly off-character.

jokes.text: So then they'll owe me something! I'm with you!
jokes.setup: Carlton's comment about girls getting food
jokes.punchline: Will immediately sees transactional dating opportunity
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy showing Will's opportunistic mindset. The enthusiasm sells it well.

jokes.text: God, how I love life on the edge.
jokes.setup: Carlton acknowledging the stock tip is illegal
jokes.punchline: Carlton thinks small-scale insider trading makes him a dangerous rebel
jokes.explanation: Perfect Carlton delusion - his idea of 'living dangerously' is adorably sheltered. Great character integration.

jokes.text: Those almost look like real diamonds. / They are, so be careful.
jokes.setup: Carlton examining Aunt Viv's bracelet dismissively
jokes.punchline: Aunt Viv reveals they ARE real diamonds, making Carlton look foolish
jokes.explanation: Classic misdirection joke that sets up Carlton's later panic when they lose the bracelet.

jokes.text: My, that's a lovely tattoo. You know, the knife dripping blood really sets off your eyes.
jokes.setup: Carlton trying to be polite to tough pawn shop owner Agnes
jokes.punchline: Carlton's refined manners applied to describing violent tattoo imagery
jokes.explanation: Excellent fish-out-of-water comedy. Carlton's preppy politeness describing gruesome tattoo imagery is perfectly incongruous.

jokes.text: The one-armed chick that works the corner of Hollywood and Vine?
jokes.setup: Carlton mentions Catherine the Great as former bracelet owner
jokes.punchline: Agnes thinks he means a street prostitute with the same name
jokes.explanation: Great cultural clash humor - high culture reference meets street reality. The specificity of 'one-armed' makes it funnier.

jokes.text: She a class act. Okay, $300.
jokes.setup: Agnes identifying the street prostitute Catherine
jokes.punchline: Agnes deadpan endorsement of the prostitute's quality
jokes.explanation: Good deadpan follow-up that extends the cultural clash. Agnes's matter-of-fact endorsement is funny.

jokes.text: Moron! Fathead!
jokes.setup: Geoffrey seemingly responding graciously to Philip's compliment
jokes.punchline: Geoffrey is actually yelling at the baseball game on radio
jokes.explanation: Perfect misdirection - audience thinks Geoffrey is insulting Philip, but it's the baseball game. Great economy and timing.

jokes.text: Bunt, you pasty-faced sod!
jokes.setup: Geoffrey promising to maintain professionalism during dinner
jokes.punchline: Immediately breaks character again with more creative baseball insults
jokes.explanation: Good callback that escalates Geoffrey's British insults. 'Pasty-faced sod' is particularly funny coming from the butler.

jokes.text: Mom, have I told you that every year you grow even more youthful? You're going to be well into your 40s before you need plastic surgery.
jokes.setup: Hilary seemingly complimenting her mother's youthfulness
jokes.punchline: Backhanded compliment implies Vivian will inevitably need surgery
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hilary - vapid compliment that's actually an insult. The 'well into your 40s' timing makes it worse.

jokes.text: Men with money can drive her away in their cars.
jokes.setup: Discussion about Diamond League women caring about cars, men, and money
jokes.punchline: Philip's literal interpretation creating unintended scenario
jokes.explanation: Dad joke territory but fits Philip's character. Simple wordplay that's mildly amusing but not clever.

jokes.text: You never got me a pony.
jokes.setup: Family discussion about the Diamond League lunch
jokes.punchline: Hilary brings up completely unrelated childhood grievance as leverage
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Hilary's spoiled logic - random guilt trip about a pony. Great economy.

jokes.text: What the hell's your problem? You got bricks in your butt, you clown?
jokes.setup: Geoffrey seemingly responding to Vivian's request to buff silver
jokes.punchline: Geoffrey is still yelling at the baseball game with increasingly creative insults
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation of Geoffrey's baseball rage. 'Bricks in your butt' is wonderfully absurd and British-flavored.

jokes.text: I'm gonna go out and get my very own team. It's between the Raiderettes and the Laker Girls.
jokes.setup: Will planning what to do with his money
jokes.punchline: Will casually discusses buying cheerleader teams like choosing between cars
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy showing Will's materialistic fantasies. The casual tone makes it funnier.

jokes.text: You can't buy love, Will. / Man, what you talking about? I don't want love. I just want them to follow me around saying, 'Go, Will. Get busy. Put your thing down.'
jokes.setup: Carlton's earnest advice about not buying love
jokes.punchline: Will completely misses the point and describes wanting cheerleaders as hype men
jokes.explanation: Excellent character contrast - Carlton's wisdom vs Will's shallow desires. The specific chants are perfectly Will.

jokes.text: How can I put this in terms he'll understand? More money.
jokes.setup: Will asking Carlton to translate SEC news
jokes.punchline: Carlton's condescending 'translation' is just stating the obvious
jokes.explanation: Good Carlton pomposity - his condescending explanation is hilariously simple. Great economy.

jokes.text: See, I'm gonna go out and get me a Burger King, right? And then I'm gonna hire some topless counter girls... and then I'm gonna really have it my way.
jokes.setup: Will planning business investments with his money
jokes.punchline: Will turns Burger King's slogan into sexual innuendo combined with his strip club concept
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay combining corporate slogan with Will's fantasies. The buildup to the pun payoff works well.

jokes.text: Carlton? / Yes, Will? / That ain't a good thing, is it? / No, Will. / We're in trouble, ain't we? / Yes, Will.
jokes.setup: News about Chapter 11 bankruptcy filing
jokes.punchline: Will's gradual realization delivered as scared child-like questions, Carlton's defeated responses
jokes.explanation: Great timing and structure showing their growing panic. The childlike question-answer format is perfectly executed.

jokes.text: No Harvard. Dead end. 'Paper or plastic?'
jokes.setup: Carlton panicking about losing money
jokes.punchline: Carlton's worst fear is working at a grocery store
jokes.explanation: Perfect Carlton character moment - his privileged horror at the prospect of working retail. Great escalating panic.

jokes.text: $24, $25. Gosh. Who'd have thought they'd pay you so little for selling your blood?
jokes.setup: Boys are desperately trying to make money
jokes.punchline: Carlton's naive surprise at low blood bank compensation
jokes.explanation: Perfect Carlton sheltered perspective on desperate measures. His polite surprise at exploitation economics.

jokes.text: You only have to give blood 50 more times to get Mom's bracelet back.
jokes.setup: Carlton trying to be optimistic about their blood donation earnings
jokes.punchline: The math reveals how hopeless their situation is
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist math humor that highlights their desperation. Carlton's optimistic delivery makes it funnier.

jokes.text: But I see three of you, and I don't know which one to hit.
jokes.setup: Carlton asking if Will would hit him with the newspaper
jokes.punchline: Will is so weak from blood loss he's seeing triple
jokes.explanation: Good physical comedy callback to their blood donation. Will's weakness creates the visual gag.

jokes.text: What are you qualified to do besides flirt with women... and dance to loud, primitive music?
jokes.setup: Carlton dismissing Will's job prospects
jokes.punchline: Carlton's snobbish description of Will's street skills
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy showing Carlton's racial/class prejudices dressed as practical concern. 'Primitive music' is particularly snooty.

jokes.text: Male strippers wanted. Bingo.
jokes.setup: Will searching classifieds for work matching his skills
jokes.punchline: Will finds the perfect job that combines both his 'qualifications'
jokes.explanation: Perfect payoff to Carlton's insults - Will finds a job that literally requires flirting and dancing. Great callback structure.

jokes.text: Shake that groove thing.
jokes.setup: Carlton reluctantly accepting the stripping plan
jokes.punchline: Carlton awkwardly trying to be supportive with dated slang
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - Carlton trying to be cool with extremely uncool language choice.

jokes.text: Does David Duke shop white sales?
jokes.setup: Carlton asking if Will got the stripper job
jokes.punchline: Will's rhetorical question combining the racist leader with 'white sales' double meaning
jokes.explanation: Excellent wordplay combining current events with retail terminology. Smart, topical, and perfectly delivered as obvious rhetorical question.

jokes.text: Dancing around in some cheap club... while women put dollars down my underpants. I love this country.
jokes.setup: Will considering the degrading aspects of stripping
jokes.punchline: Will concludes this represents American opportunity at its finest
jokes.explanation: Great patriotic twist on a degrading situation. Will's ability to find the American Dream in stripping is perfect character work.

jokes.text: They're not, thieving wankers.
jokes.setup: Geoffrey explaining his baseball radio outbursts
jokes.punchline: Geoffrey calls the players 'thieving wankers' with British dignity
jokes.explanation: Good callback to Geoffrey's baseball rage. 'Thieving wankers' is a perfect British insult for baseball players.

jokes.text: I'm Shamu.
jokes.setup: Philip complaining about his weight loss program failure
jokes.punchline: Philip compares himself to the famous whale
jokes.explanation: Solid self-deprecating humor. Simple but effective comparison with great economy.

jokes.text: She's going to make some man a wonderful wife one day. Soon, I hope.
jokes.setup: Mrs. DeWynter apparently complimenting Hilary
jokes.punchline: Reveals her eagerness to get rid of Hilary through marriage
jokes.explanation: Classic passive-aggressive socialite behavior. The 'soon, I hope' tag reveals true feelings beneath politeness.

jokes.text: That's what Drew Barrymore said.
jokes.setup: Ashley claiming she can take care of herself at 13
jokes.punchline: Vivian references Drew Barrymore's child star troubles as cautionary tale
jokes.explanation: Sharp topical reference that's both funny and makes parenting point. Great economy and relevance.

jokes.text: No, Master William's not home, thank God.
jokes.setup: Geoffrey answering phone professionally
jokes.punchline: Geoffrey adds his personal relief that Will isn't home
jokes.explanation: Good Geoffrey moment showing his true feelings about Will while maintaining professional facade.

jokes.text: And they also requested that you bring a spare... G-string.
jokes.setup: Geoffrey delivering Will's job requirements professionally
jokes.punchline: Geoffrey's discomfort saying 'G-string' breaks his usual composure
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment showing Geoffrey's discomfort with Will's career choice. The pause sells it.

jokes.text: Gingham Turtle restaurant. Sounds pretty sleazy.
jokes.setup: Carlton reading the address for Will's stripping gig
jokes.punchline: Carlton thinks the wholesome-sounding name indicates a seedy venue
jokes.explanation: Great misdirection - the innocent name will contrast with Carlton's expectations. Good setup for the family restaurant reveal.

jokes.text: Check out the lowlife.
jokes.setup: Will arriving at what he thinks is a strip club
jokes.punchline: Will sees upscale diners as 'lowlife' because he's expecting sleazy clientele
jokes.explanation: Good ironic reversal - Will's expectations are completely wrong about the venue and clientele.

jokes.text: Between King Thong and Rambro.
jokes.setup: Will asking about other male strippers
jokes.punchline: Will mentions made-up stripper names that parody action heroes
jokes.explanation: Solid parody names for strippers - 'Rambro' especially works as Rambo parody.

jokes.text: Sir Graham Higgins, doing a medley of operatic favorites.
jokes.setup: Announcement of evening entertainment
jokes.punchline: The 'entertainment' is opera, not stripping - complete misdirection
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection payoff - the audience realizes Will is at the wrong venue entirely. Perfect setup for the chaos to follow.

jokes.text: Will... shake something, damn it!
jokes.setup: Will performing opera in drag to confused family audience
jokes.punchline: Hilary breaks character and yells for Will to strip
jokes.explanation: Brilliant payoff moment - Hilary's reaction is perfect character comedy and the culmination of the misunderstanding. Highly quotable and memorable.

jokes.text: Take it off! / Put it on!
jokes.setup: Confused crowd not understanding what's happening
jokes.punchline: Crowd gives contradictory instructions as situation devolves
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing the chaos and confusion of the situation.

jokes.text: Take it off! Put it on!
jokes.setup: Continued chaos at the restaurant
jokes.punchline: Contradictory shouting showing complete confusion of situation
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing complete chaos. Contradictory demands highlight how wrong everything has gone.

jokes.text: Carlton Banks, you put your clothes on this minute! / Mommy.
jokes.setup: Vivian discovering Carlton stripping at the family restaurant
jokes.punchline: Carlton's childlike response when caught by his mother
jokes.explanation: Perfect cringe moment - Carlton reverting to childhood when caught by mom. Great character consistency.

jokes.text: I just wanna say it's all his fault.
jokes.setup: Carlton apologizing for the stripping incident
jokes.punchline: Carlton immediately throws Will under the bus
jokes.explanation: Perfect Carlton character moment - abandoning any loyalty to save himself. Very on-brand.

jokes.text: Federal offense. Go on.
jokes.setup: Will confessing about the insider trading
jokes.punchline: Philip calmly cataloging their crimes
jokes.explanation: Excellent deadpan reaction showing Philip's legal mind categorizing their crimes matter-of-factly.

jokes.text: Grand larceny. Impressive.
jokes.setup: Will confessing about pawning the bracelet
jokes.punchline: Philip continues his deadpan legal analysis with sarcastic approval
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of Philip's deadpan crime cataloging. 'Impressive' adds the perfect sarcastic touch.

jokes.text: I don't know. Reward us for our honesty?
jokes.setup: Philip asking what punishment they deserve
jokes.punchline: Will optimistically suggests they should be rewarded instead
jokes.explanation: Good Will logic - trying to spin their confession into a positive. Shows his street-smart attempts at manipulation.

jokes.text: Hey, it worked for the Beave.
jokes.setup: Will justifying his reward suggestion
jokes.punchline: Will references Leave it to Beaver's moral lessons
jokes.explanation: Nice TV reference showing Will's pop culture knowledge. Sets up Philip's comeback perfectly.

jokes.text: The Beave! Do I look like a white guy named Ward?
jokes.setup: Will's Leave it to Beaver reference
jokes.punchline: Philip points out the racial reality of their family dynamic vs 1950s TV
jokes.explanation: Excellent social commentary disguised as a punchline. Philip's reality check about race and family expectations is both funny and pointed.

jokes.text: The Beave! Do I look like a white guy named Ward?
jokes.setup: Will's reference to Leave it to Beaver family dynamics
jokes.punchline: Philip points out racial/cultural differences from 1950s TV family
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback that addresses racial dynamics of classic TV. Philip's reality check on outdated family sitcom expectations.

jokes.text: I thought we had an understanding, you know? I mean, we speak the same language and all. What's going on? / Babe, we ain't nothing going on but the rent.
jokes.setup: Will trying to appeal to Agnes based on shared background
jokes.punchline: Agnes shuts down Will's attempt at cultural solidarity with pure business focus
jokes.explanation: Great character moment showing Agnes's no-nonsense business approach trumping any cultural connection. 'Nothing going on but the rent' is perfectly delivered.

jokes.text: Hey, look, Agnes, slimmie, now, what's up? How you gonna play me?
jokes.setup: Will tries to negotiate with tough pawn shop owner
jokes.punchline: Uses street slang and casual tone with dangerous person
jokes.explanation: Will's street persona attempting to establish connection with Agnes. 'Slimmie' and casual approach show his confidence.

jokes.text: Babe, we ain't nothing going on but the rent.
jokes.setup: Will trying to appeal to street solidarity with Agnes
jokes.punchline: Agnes shuts him down with superior street credentials
jokes.explanation: Agnes out-streets Will with better vernacular. 'Nothing going on but the rent' is pure business, deflating his charm attempt.

jokes.text: I hope it doesn't interfere with our relationship, Sweet 'N Low.
jokes.setup: Agnes explaining business comes first over any personal connection
jokes.punchline: Condescending pet name emphasizing Carlton's small stature and weakness
jokes.explanation: Perfect emasculating nickname for Carlton. 'Sweet 'N Low' combines condescension with reference to his size and artificial sweetness.

jokes.text: Work it, honey.
jokes.setup: Boys forced to dance for bracelet
jokes.punchline: Agnes encouraging their humiliating performance
jokes.explanation: Callback to earlier stripping theme but in pawn shop context. Agnes enjoying their desperation and humiliation.

jokes.text: Hey, it worked for the Beave. / Worked for the Beave! Do I look like a white guy named Ward?
jokes.setup: Identical setup to earlier scene - Will referencing Leave it to Beaver
jokes.punchline: Philip gives identical response, showing this is a repeated family dynamic
jokes.explanation: Effective callback that shows this is recurring family pattern. Less impact than original but good structural choice for episode ending.
